channel.title: George Rice and the First International Polar Year Expedition

item.dc:creator: George W Rice
item.description:
1881-07-29

We left Upernavik today at 4:30 p.m. All hands were busy in writing letters before starting. These go by a Danish ship which will soon leave Greenland for Copenhagen. I sent letter to Herald.

Inspector and Governor Elberg came and bad us farewell, not leaving the ship until we had passed out of the harbor.

1881-07-30

We found very little ice. It has been the experience of almost all voyagers to fall in with the ice pack after leaving Upernavik1. Our departure furnished us much food for reflection. Upernavik is the last point at which the least semblance of civilization can be found. Our course now leads us to that vague, unknown region which is so indefinitely marked out as to be undefined. Our ship now plows waters that have seldom been disturbed. No sails whiten the broad expanse of dark blue water. In their stead, the stately bergs move majestically along. One can scarcely refrain from descanting on the beauties of these bergs whenever the subject is suggested. To think of them only as immense islands of ice conveys no idea of their remarkable beauty when lighted up by the peculiar rays of the midnight sun.

Today, when in latitude about 74° 34’, considerable excitement was occasioned by the discovery of a veritable polar bear. We had steamed into a small pack of ice extending in a narrow belt and his bearship was seen on one of the pieces of ice enjoying an evening meal off a seal he had caught. A Bear! A Bear! was shouted in excited tones all over the ship, and soon our hunters were seen emergine with their rifles. The bear was a few hundred yards from the ship and from a number of shots fired one or two bullets hit him. He had hitherto been perfectly indifferent to our approach, but, finding it disagreeable for him, he took to the water, swam a distance, and emerged to crawl upon the ice again. By this time a boat was lowered and Lieuts. Kislingbury and Lockwood with Mr. Clay rowed nearer, despatched him, and brought the body triumphantly aboard. As soon as he was hoisted on deck and placed in position, I proceeded to make a photograph of him. It was a young make and proved to be in fine condition. Our two Esquimaux were in their element dressing the carcase, and soon had it hanging up.

1881-07-31

We are making good progress north. It is considered strange that no large pack of ice has been met. We fell in today with a small pack, but soon steamed through it. Several seals of the square flipper kind were seen on the ice and we succeeded in shooting one, which I photographed when brought on board.

The bear meat which we had for breakfast proved quite palatable, although tasting a little fishy, the ducks have reconciled us that taste; the bear was not more fishy than they.

Our faire will be varied still further by the meat of the seal, which is considered excellent.

1881-08-01

The fog has been setting down but fortunately cleared away by the time we sighted Cape Dudley Digges. We soon after made the Carey Islands, and the Proteus stood in to the land, remaining about three hours. A large party of us landed. The cairn erected by Capt Allen Young was examined and a record found, which we brought away, leaving a copy to which Lieut. Greely added a short account of our own progress. A letter addressed to Sir George Nares was also found. While we were at the cairn another division of the party found and examined the cache of stores or rations left by Sir George. Trey were in good condition. The rum was tested and pronounced excellent. Several cans of the meat were brought on board, and appeared to be as good as when deposited six years ago. The biscuit was, however, mouldy in part about one fourth being spoiled.

1881-08-02

This morning we had the pleasure of sighting Cape Alexander, at the entrance of Smith Sound. We soon steamed by, passing the celebrated Crystal Palace glacier and Crystal Palace cliffs. Cape Alexander is in latitude 78° 10’, and, as it is considered the key to the unknown Arctic, considerable exultation was felt. “We are within eighteen degrees of the pole,” said Lieut. Greely, “if we never get any nearer,”. Soon we passed Port Foulke, Dr. Hayes winter quarters, and anchored about noon by Littleton Island. Capt. Allen Young, in the Pandora, left letters here for the Alert and Discovery, which were recovered by these ships as they were on their way home, and were south of the place when Capt Young deposited. It was also about this place that the ill starred Polaris was lost, and on the mainland the party, under Capt. Buddington, wintered.

Footnotes

  1. It’s interesting that this is the second time that George comments on the amount of ice they have encountered. And knowing the rest of the story, that relief ships were not able to reach the expedition in subsequent years due to too much ice. So George and company had no issues with the Proteus getting as far north as they did due to an unusually warm summer, and lack of sea ice. But the next years, there was a normal (I’m not 100% sure) amount of ice, and that meant their extra supplies couldn’t get to them, nor could a ship easily reach them for rescue.↩︎

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

Citation

BibTeX citation:
@online{w rice2024,
  author = {W Rice, George},
  title = {1881-07-29:1881-08-02},
  date = {2024-03-03},
  url = {https://rmflight.github.io/george_rice_polar_expedition/Diary_Entries/1881/1881-07-29},
  langid = {en}
}
For attribution, please cite this work as:

item.dc:creator: George W Rice
item.description:
1881-07-21

Left Disco at 2 a.m. and, after a run of about sixty miles along the coast of Disco Island, arrived at Rittentank. We were welcomed by a salute of three guns from the battery in front of the governor’s residence. Mr. Clay came on board looking none the worse for his winter in the arctic. Lieut Lockwood, with a small party, visited the “loomery”, seven miles from here, and brought back seventy birds, principally the small auk. He reports great numbers of birds. A great many were shot that could not be picked up on account of their inaccessible position on the high cliffs. Many eggs were destroyed by the birds dragging them off in their fall from their nests on the shelving rocks.

We have taken seven dogs in addition to those already procured. Also, about two tons of the dried fish for food. Dr. Parry and H. Clay are busy getting their effects on board.

1881-07-22

Left Rittentank this p.m. for Upernavik. Will pass through the Waigett, the strait separating Disco Island from the mainland.

1881-07-23

On the water. Still in the Waigett. The scenery through this strait is most magnificent. The land is within sight most of the way. The frowning cliffs rise abruptly from the sea, which, calmed by the numerous icebergs, is as placid as the waters of Central Park, and reflects back the cliffs and the snow clad mountains behind them. The stately bergs present the most beautiful feature of the panorama. Immense islands of ice, pure and white as Parlan marble awaiting the chisel fo the sculptor, although no artist could give them forms more beautiful or fantastic. They resemble at times the mosques and minarets of some oriental city, when the midnight sun dyes them in glorious tins. The base is generally marked with a beautiful green where the dark sea water washes over it.

1881-07-24

Arrived at Upernavik this morning at 5:30. The latitude of this place is 72° 48’ north. It resembles the other Danish settlements we have visited: Consists of the residence of the Governor, the storehouse, cooper’s shop, smithy, and the turf huts of the Esquimaux population. Governor Elberg has received us very graciously, and expresses his willingness to aid the expedition all he can. The Inspector of North Greenland, whom we saw at Disco, is here temporarily on his tour of inspection. We shall take on more dogs here also skins and skin clothing.

1881-07-25

It is blowing hard this morning and a party that was to leave for Proven did not start. The dogs have been landed on a small rock near the harbor, that they may get some exercise. Expect to be here about five days.

1881-07-26

A party – Lieut. Lockwood, Gov. Elberg, Cross, Linn, and myself, – started this morning for Proven to procure two Esquimaux to accompany the expedition as dog drivers and hunters. The distance is about fifty miles. We took the usual passage through the islands, as the sea was too rough to go outside. Our conveyance was the little steam launch, “Lady Greely”. The scenery in the channels and fiords through which we passes was most remarkable and on the same grand and impressive scale as that of the Waigett. Some of the passages were so narrow that, at first sight, it appeared impossible to get through them; but nearer approach proved them to be as deep as mid ocean. The walls of the adamantine cliffs descend perpendicularly to a great depth. Nothing but rock, evidently of volcanic origin, appears. Occasionally a little carpeting of beautiful green moss covers the avidences of nature’s convulsions, and t is the only representative of the vegetable kingdom that we can see. We arrived at Proven at 9:30 p.m.

1881-07-27

At Proven better opportunity for studying the habits of the aborigines is afforded than at any other place we have visited. The whalers and exploring parties seldom get there; so the influence of the white man is less perceptible. They retain their habits of life as practiced before the Dane improved their condition, to a greater extent than in other parts of Greenland. I spent at least three hours in one of the most uninviting igloos or huts that I saw. The manufacture of a cap of eider down, for which I was waiting, gave me pretext for so long a stay. The hut was built of turf, rocks, and moss and was entered through a long tunnel so low that I was compelled to crawl on my hands and feet to effect an entrance, a most disagreeable and humiliating proceeding, as the dogs and the natives (the latter not much better than the former in point of cleanliness,) enter by the same way. On reaching the small interior hole, which is at once the living, eating and sleeping room, I was surprised to see the mistress of the house in a state of almost complete nudity, sitting on the shelf or platform on which they sleep. I turned aside to give her an opportunity of donning a more complete costume, but a furtive glance soon showed me that she appeared to prefer being dresses as nearly a la Venus as possible.

I at once faced the music and had soon made a satisfactory arrangement by which I was to receive a cap in exchange for a neckerchief I wore. The cap was not in esse, so the contract remained executory until it was completed.

The maker measured the dome of my intellect with great pleasure, and was soon engaged in cutting up eider skins and fitting them into the form of a cap. Her scantiness of clothes appeared to give her an advantage as she seemed to get along more expeditiously by odling the material between her feet while sewing.

The room was very low, and the atmosphere stifling and nauseating from the odors of the skins and oil. There was no glass in the window. As a substitute they used the the membraneous tissue tissue of the stomach of the seal, which, when oiled, is almost transparent. There was no stove nor fireplace. Instead, they used a hollow stone for a lamp with fuel of oil and moss for a wick. The floor was of stone and earth. The sleeping arrangements were very simple. A platform extending six fee from the wall occupies one side of the room. This is the receptacle of skins and furs during the day, and, when the natives retire the make it a general couch; each disciple of Morpheus drawing over himself or herself, so many of the skins as are necessary for comfort.

The costume of the Esquimaux women is so nearly like that of the men that, at first sight, it is not easy to distinguish the sexes. The articles of attire of the women, however, are made up in a much more elaborate manner than are those of the men, showing more ornamentation. The feet are encased in dainty boots reaching above the knee. Next come the pants or trunks, which are very short, coming down not more than half the length of the thigh, the boots being pulled up to meet them. The upper part of the body is covered in a cassock of seal skin, devoid of buttons and pulled on over the head, or allowed to fall back at the shoulders at pleasure.

My cap being complete, I was glad to get away from the nauseating surrounding and breathe the fresh air again. Many of the natives, however, occupy much better dwellings than the one I have described. Such a one represents the habitations of the poorer class, who have not intermixed with the Danes. In the evening I entered a larger building occupied by a half breed, and, finding an old violin on the wall, I soon had a number of the fair sex dancing enthusiastically to my rather imperfect music. I became very popular with them at once, and, in leaving Proven, which did at 10:30 p.m., a bevy of dark maidens were at the shore to bid me Goodby or the Esquimaux equivalent. Through the efforts of Gov. Elberg, the two Esquimaux Christiansen and Edwards, were induced to accompany us. The exhibited considerable emotion, proving themselves very unlike their stoical North American (Indian) relatives. On parting with their friends, the shed bitter tears, that fell indeed until their homes were out of sight.

We had a very pleasant passage returning. The midnight sun made it light as day. We shot one hundred and thirty looms, or, at least, we recovered that many; large numbers remaining dead on the cliffs inaccessible to us. The females were sitting in rows, incubating, and, when shot, the eggs would roll off, and come crashing down the rocks in thousands, turning the water yellow. The two natives were in their element, darting about in their graceful kyaks, picking up the dead birds.

1881-07-28

We arrived at Upernavik, reaching the ship at about 10:30 a.m. Took dogs on board the Proteus. Their number is now thirty-four grown, and three pups. I am busy the remaining part of the day photographing the Inspector and the Governor’s children, and in getting my things packed away before starting to sea again.

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

Citation

  title = {1881-07-21:1881-07-28},
  date = {2024-03-02},

  langid = {en}

item.dc:creator: George W Rice
item.description:
1881-07-17

This morning we are informed that a boat leaves here for Egadesminda, where a Danish ship is lying about ready to start for Copenhagen and will carry our mail matter, which can be sent to the United States, via Denmark. We are all therefore busily engaged in writing to friends and relatives. I send communication to the New York Herald, reporting our arrival and stating our chances for getting through to Lady Franklin Bay, as well as we can judge from the outlook here. The day is a very fine one. The bell in the primitive little mission church is calling the little community to worship. Some of the men attend. Snyder volunteers his services as organist to the small instrument which stands in one corner of the church. After the services is over he pleases the Esquimaux very much by playing them some lively music, soon attracting a much larger congregation than had attended the worship. His music, which was interspersed with an occasional jib and dancing tune, was rather too secular to be appropriate for the Sabbath, but we are compelled to admit that the natives were more pleased with it than with the preceding devotional exercises. I meet the same faces I saw here last year. Mr. Smith is still Inspector; Governor Fliescher is Trader, and there has been no material change in the Esquimaux population. The are all pleased with our ship which they consider “Much Strong.”

1881-07-18

It is difficult for one not noting the time to distinguish night and day here. The sun does not set till about 11 p.m., and is up again at 1 a.m. The evening and morning twilight blend into one, casting a silvery, misty light over everything. I am writing this in the cabin at midnight, without the aid of artificial lights. The crew have been busy all day, shifting the cargo and putting more coal in the bunkers. Our men are bringing off the lumber that was left by the Gulnare, also the pemmican stores, etc. It has been too foggy for me to make photographs of Disco or the ship. On the day we arrived here we noticed a magnificent iceberg near the mouth of the harbor. It was in the form of an immense arch, and presented a very impressive appearance. It was my intention to return and photograph it at the first opportunity, but we had barely reached an anchorage before it fell to pieces, with a terrific crash louder than thunder. The sea was disturbed for miles.

1881-07-19

I make several negatives today; photograph the Proteus and Godhaven Harbor. I also make a group of the Governor’s family and Miss Smith and her governess. They are very much pleased to have the opportunity of being photographed, and dress themselves for the occasion with great care. Miss Smith and her companion look particularly charming in the bright lively colors of the Danish peasant girl. At least, they appeared very charming to us, who do not expect to see another woman in a civilized costume for two years.

Lieut Greely has purchased a quantity of white whale skin for food. It is an excellent antiscorbutic, and is said to resemble tripe in flavor. It is in a dried stated. He also has contracted for dried fish and walrus meat, which is to be the food of the dogs. We will take in about fourteen dogs here and will get others at Rittentank and Upernavik. Inspector Smith and Governor Fliescher are aiding our commander in every possible way.

1881-07-20

Took aboard fourteen dogs, with dog food for their consumption. The walrus meat is most disagreeable in odor, as it is partially decomposed, but appears to be relished by the dogs. Getting the animals on board was an exciting operation. They offered every resistance and howled and yelped in a most discordant and disagreeable manner. They are now penned in a small space on deck where they snarl and snap at one another in a most unfriendly way. Several encounters between the more warlike of the males have settled the question of supremacy, and now one battle-scarred old veteran struts around, master of the situation.

Dr. Perry arrived from Rittentank today, looking well. He reports H. Clay still in Rittentank in good health.

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

Citation

BibTeX citation:
@online{w rice2024,
  author = {W Rice, George},
  title = {1881-07-17:1881-07-20},
  date = {2024-03-02},

  langid = {en}
}

item.dc:creator: George W Rice
item.description:
1881-07-07

For our transportation to Lady Franklin Bay the United States government has chartered the sealing steamer Proteus, owned and hailing from St. John’s, Newfoundland. She is commanded by Capt. Pike, who has for his first officer, Mr. Norman; second, Mr. White. Mr. McPherson is first engineer, and Mr. Carmicheal, second. The crew is composed of sturdy Newfoundland sailors. Capt. Pike and his mates have all had long experience in ice navigation, and the ship is specially adapted for her mission, as she was built for the seal fishing, and everything that experience could suggest was done to make capable of resisting the action of ice.

This morning the freight for the expedition, that arrived per Hibernian, was brought on board the Proteus and every preparation made for a start. At noon Captain Pike gave the order to get under weigh. About 12:30 we move from the anchorage, and amid cheers and the waving of handkerchiefs from the shore steam slowly through the narrows. Much interest is taken in our departure and it is with feelings of regret that we gave for the last time for many days upon that most interesting feature of society and civilized life represented by a group of gaily dressed ladies who stood on Signal Hill and kindly waved us “good by”. Bleak and cold as Newfoundland looks, with its drapery of fog hanging over its high cliffs, we shall exchange it for the lands of eternal snow with regret.

Our start is a favorable one and we go coasting along the shore under both sail and steam at a rapid rate. The wind is a pleasant breeze and we are soon carried past the entrance to the great bays, Conception and Trinity. The crew are busily engaged cleaning up the decks and “making things ship shape” as the mate says. Our party occupied their time in locating, doffing their city costumes, and donning clothes more substantial and better adapted for ship life. Some are evidently meditating on the future fo the arduous undertaking toward which we have taken the initial step today and which has probably, in many cases, never been so seriously considered as at this moment. I realize that we have left behind us relatives, friends, society, civilization and every thing that makes life pleasant on this terren sphere, for the long space of two years, is enough to furnish ample food for reflection even to the most thoughtless. And then, it is impossible to avoid looking forward with something like apprehension to the future sojourn in those regions of perpetual snow and ice under a hostile sky.

No matter how much forethought may have been exercised thse thoughts have never so strongly suggested themselves as at this time when we find ourselves on the broad Atlantic with the prow of our ship pointed toward the north star and every revolution of the propeller speeding us nearer our inhospitable destination and further from home and friends. We retire at a late hour to be lulled to sleep by the motion of the ship, which has not yet become unpleasant, and dream of icebergs and polar bears.

1881-07-08

We are bowling along at a splendid rate. The weather is fair but the temperature is growing much cooler. Now that we are all together a roll call to make acquaintance is not amiss:

We are members of an Arctic exploring expedition sent out under the auspices of the War Department of the United States of America (or, more immediately, of the Chief Signal Officer, U.S.A.) “for scientific observation in the Arctic regions.”1 The plan of the expedition is to establish a station at Lady Franklin Bay, latitude 81°, 40’ and spend two years in observation and exploration, making journeys by sledge to the north at such times and seasons as practicable. The expedition is under the command of Lieut. A. W. Greely, and the following are the names of the persons comprising the party: Lieut A.W. Greely, in command; Lieut Kislingbury; Israel, astronomer; Gardiner, astronomer, Jewell, meteorologist; Ralston, meteorologist; Rice, photographer.

1881-07-09

There is a strong breeze from the north-west which makes our progress very slow. The temperature is getting very much cooler.

1881-07-10

Our first Sunday at sea: A pleasant day. The wind has changed and is now in our favor, wafting us along, with the aid of steam, at a splendid rate.

1881-07-11

At four p.m. wind hauled ahead, increasing in force until it now threatens to become a gale. No observation has been made.

1881-07-12

The wind is still adverse. The temperature has fallen very low and indicates the presence of ice. At 8 p.m. we fall in with the ice, a long narrow strip of field ice, through which we carefully steam at half speed, following the tortuous channel which the opening in the ice affords.

The crow’s nest has been hoisted up to the fore-top-gallant-mast head, and in it the second mate stands and directs the movements of the ship. The presence of the ice has calmed the sea, and we are surrounded with a most beautiful scene. The blue-green edges of the ice, blending with the dark blue ocean an lighted up by the oblique rays of the setting sun, present a panorama of rate beauty, resembling a mosaic on a grand scale.

In the foreground can be seen several seals of the harp species. Lieut. Kislingbury hazards several shots at them but without effect; the distance is too great. Occasionally a mammoth whale may be seen emerging, like some miniature island among the ice, to blow, sending a spout of water, which, dyed with the tints of the setting sun, presents all the colors of the rainbow. Our position can only be ascertained by dead reckoning as no meridian observation has been made. We place this floe of ice in latitude 61° north.

1881-07-13

Our party, or those have been so unfortunate as to be sea sick, are now recovering their spirits in this water, kept smooth by the ice. This mal de mer has visited our party pretty generally, without regard to age or station. I am so fortunate as always to enjoy an immunity from it. Lieut. Greely has experienced the unpleasant effects in a large degree.

We fall in with another ice pack in about latitude 63°. It presents the same appearance as the former pack we met. The wind blowing off it makes the temperature very cold, making us realize that we must expect to invade Boress’ chilly heath earlier than we anticipated.

[missing] winds have been from the north, which augurs well for our success, as the ice will be driven out of Smith Sound, admitting our passing through it. Today we sighted land for the first time since leaving St. John’s.

1881-07-14

We have been out one week today. The sea is calm. A dense fog hangs over everything. We are compelled to go at half speed for fear of encountering bergs, which cannot be recognized at any distance through the hazy fog. The captain proudly relates an instance of the ability of the good Proteus: Which out on a sealing voyage she had just extricated herself from a pack and had attained about the rate of half speed, when, before it could be avoided, she ran head on to a large berg. She actually ran several feet up the side of the berg, so great had been the momentum. She however, sustained no other injury than the destruction of her head gear and the breaking off of the outwater. Such anecdotes of her prowess naturally inspire me with much confidence. The fog lifts about one p.m. giving us another glimpse of the snow clad mountains, nestling along the seacoast.

1881-07-15

We can occasionally see the land when the fog lifts. The captain and Mr. Israel both were successful in getting meridian observations. Our latitude is 66°, 42’ N. Land well in sight, I recognized the land very readily from my recollection of last year’s cruise. To a stranger from the south the weird appearance of these latitudes is most remarkable and fascinating. A dark, bold, striking coastline beyond which the snow clad mountains, ascending to dizzy attitudes, can be seen as the gauzy curtain of fog lifts. The sea, dotted with bergs, presents no other obstruction to the eye; and, as we again turn our eyes to land, we are impressed with the desolate and silent appearance of everything. No sign of animal or vegetable life disturbs or beautifies the scene. Silence reigns supreme.

1881-07-16

The fog has again settled down this morning, and as we are supposed to be in the vicinity of Disco, the speed is lessened. We are, it is reckoned, very near Godhaven at 2 p.m., and the engines are stopped, as it is useless to attempt to make the land, owing to the dense fog which still prevails.

At eight p.m., to our great delight, the fog, like some huge curtain, rolled back, showing the snow clad island of Disco close by. As the fog dispersed, it presented a most beautiful sight. The dark coast, plentifully dotted with glistening bergs, the high land covered with snow, an occasional stream of ice water trickling its way, like some silver ribbon, over the ice and rocks to the sea below, and the fog, like a halo, draping the highest peaks. But as we drew nearer, no sign of sunny summer could be seen, except a few patches of hardy grass struggling to grow in favored spots, and, in the valleys, the beautiful green velvety moss which gives to this land of desolation its name.

I instantly recognized the conformation of the land from my recollection of my trip in the Gulnare last year, and was able to direct the way to the harbor of Godhaven, which we soon reached, dropping anchor at about ten p.m. The sun was still up and the little basin-like harbor looked very peaceful and inviting as we [not clear] into it. It was calm and still as a pond, and, in the glassy water, which was as clear as crystal, was reflected the frowning, towering cliffs which surround it.

The picturesque Esquimaux, in their funny costumes, were soon gliding over the water toward us in their graceful, fairy like kyaks, while the pantalooned maidens were tripping down the rocks to get a nearer view of us. It is all so novel and charming that those of us who have not been here before cannot repress the greatest interest and curiosity in surroundings so strange and so unlike the civilization we have left.

Dr. Parry and Henry Clay, who were expected to join us here, are at Rittentank, distant about seventy miles. We will leave here in a few days for that place, and will thence proceed to Upernavik, our last stopping place until our destination is reached. From information Inspector Smith gives us, we infer that our chances for getting through the ice this year are very good. The winter in Greenland has been a very mild one and reports from the northern stations say that navigation is not impeded by ice.2

Footnotes

  1. See the Wikipedia article on the First International Polar Year, an international cooperative to obtain scientific observations of the Arctic by a varied group of nations.↩︎

  2. This feels like a premonition, or foreboding knowing that ice would keep their relief ships from being able to reach them with supplies or for rescue.↩︎

Reuse

CC BY 4.0

Citation

@online{w rice2024,
  author = {W Rice, George},
  title = {1881-07-07:1881-07-16},
  date = {2024-03-01},
  url = {https://rmflight.github.io/george_rice_polar_expedition/Diary_Entries/1881/1881-07-07},
  langid = {en}
}
For attribution, please cite this work as:
W Rice, George. 2024. “1881-07-07:1881-07-16.” March 1, 2024. https://rmflight.github.io/george_rice_polar_expedition/Diary_Entries/1881/1881-07-07.

item.title: Introduction
item.dc:creator: Robert M Flight
item.description:
What exactly is this project? It is a recreation of the diary of George W. Rice. George was the photographer of the expedition to Lady Franklin Bay in 1881, one of the expeditions for the First International Polar Year.

There have been books written about that expedition, and even a PBS special. That expedition, under the command of Lieut. Greely, only had 7 of the original 22 members return. George was one of the members who passed away before they were rescued. After the rescue, it appears that George’s diary was returned to his parents. The Berwick Register of Nova Scotia published extracts of his diary in the late 1800’s.

Outside of those who read the newspaper when these were originally published in the Berwick Register in the late 1800’s, and the researchers who have accessed the diary currently residing in the Dartmouth College Library special collection, I’m guessing many people have not read any of it.

George W. Rice’s family in Berwick, Nova Scotia, Canada, are a far removed branch of my Grandfather’s, Robert (Bob) E. Rice, family. Bob’s nephew (name currently escaping me), was very interested in Rice family history, and obtained a copy of the diary newspaper clippings. My Grandfather Bob made three copies of the clippings, one for each of his children. When my mother passed from early onset Alzheimer’s, I was given her copy. As far as I am aware, not even many in my family have read much of them, as the copies were very disordered. This felt like a challenging puzzle to me when I first started looking at them, so I decided to see if I could get them in the right order. Over the course of a couple of weeks, I got them in order. But the copies are still really hard to read, and I think they deserve to be shared (and the copyright is long gone on them). This is why I’m typing them up, and not just making a PDF of scans of pages.

So, if you want, you can read along as I manage to get them transcribed.

There are sections where the text is illegible or pieces of paper are missing. Those may eventually get filled in from the original, as I’ve asked for scans from Dartmouth College Libraries.

I’m not sure how often these will get updated, but I do intend to get through all of the newspaper extracts that I can (there is only 100 pages that I need to type up).

Also to note, I don’t actually know how much of the diary was printed in the newspaper. The diary was leant to the newspaper by George’s parents, and then they published what they wanted. However, the dates seem to go rather straight-forwardly along from July of 1881 to April of 1884, and they note where there are any large gaps. So I am guessing it is relatively complete.

Let’s go along with George Rice, and the rest of the expedition to the then unknown northern reaches.

The first diary entry is here. You can use the side navigation to look for specific entries.

One note about the language and attitudes expressed in this work: George was living in the late 1800’s, and the writing reflects that, especially in his writing about the native peoples. I’m not editing anything out. I do encourage you to see those, be thankful how much some attitudes have changed, and perhaps also reflect on how much more change there needs to be.
